The observed release pattern of the examined 9c/NP and 9e/NP indicated a biphasic release proﬁle (in a
without Tween 20 release medium). Speciﬁcally, there was an initial increase in drug release within the ﬁrst 6 h,
followed by a sustained release pattern throughout the studied period. At the end of 48 h, the maximum release
percentage of 9e/NPs increased to 34%, while that of 9c/NPs only increased to 23.45%. This difference between
the release percentages was associated with the fact that compound 9c is more hydrophobic than compound 9e
(more hydrophobicity provided by the extra phenyl group it contains in its structure compared to compound
9e), thus, compound 9c diffuses more difﬁcult from the PLGA matrix into the hydrophilic PBS medium
The in vitro release of 9c or 9e from nanoparticles has been shown to follow a biphasic release proﬁle with a
burst release phase and a slow steady release phase (up to 48 h) in the presence of Tween 20 in the release
medium. An initial burst release resulting from drug diffusion was observed at the conclusion of the ﬁrst hour
for 9e/NPs, similar to 9c/NPs. The burst release percentage in 9c/NPs was determined to be 50.58%, whereas in
9e/NPs it was found to be 53.38%. In the second phase of the release of 9c from 9c/NPs, an approximately
constant and low level of 9c release was observed, and at the end of 48 h, the cumulative release for 9c/NP
reached 80.52%. In the second phase of the release of 9e from 9e/NPs, a constant and low level of 9e release was
